slides.title: Values Have Types — And You Can Switch
slides.body: Every value in Python has a **type** — `int`, `str`, `float`, and more. **Type conversion** (casting) lets you change a value from one type to another so Python understands what you really mean.
slides.tip: You'll use this constantly — especially when dealing with user input.

slides.title: input() Always Returns a String
slides.body: `input()` gives you back **text**, even if the user typed a number. That means `"5"` and `5` look the same to you, but Python won't let you do math on `"5"`.
slides.code:
age = input("How old are you? ")  # user types: 25
print(type(age))
print(age + 1)
slides.output:
<class 'str'>
TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
slides.tip: The fix? Convert the value before using it.

slides.title: int() and float() Fix User Input
slides.body: Wrap `input()` in `int()` to get a whole number, or `float()` if decimals are possible. The conversion happens instantly — no extra step needed.
slides.code:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))  # user types: 25
print(age + 1)

price = float(input("Enter price: "))  # user types: 9.99
print(price * 0.1)
slides.tip: int() chops off decimals — int(3.9) is 3, not 4.

slides.title: str() Turns Numbers Into Text
slides.body: Need to join a number into a sentence? Use `str()` to convert it first — you can't concatenate a number directly onto a string with `+`.
slides.code:
score = 98
message = "Your score is " + str(score) + " out of 100!"
print(message)
slides.tip: f-strings skip this step — but str() shows you what's really happening.

slides.title: You Can Cast Between Any Types
slides.body: You now know the core toolkit: `int()`, `float()`, `str()`, and `bool()`. Just remember — converting something nonsensical like `int("hello")` raises a **ValueError**, so make sure the value makes sense first.
slides.tip: Type conversion is one of the most-used skills in everyday Python.
